inputs.contents:
Let $A$ and $B$ be two sets. Then the union of $A$ and $B$ is the set

$$A \cup B = \{x \mid x \in A \text{ or } x \in B\} \text{ .}$$

bodylist.description:
Then the union of $A$ and $B$ is the set

$$A \cup B = \{x \mid x \in A \text{ or } x \in B\} \text{ .}$$

inputs.contents:
The difference of $A$ and $B$ is the set

$$A - B = \{x \mid x \in A \text{ and } x \notin B\} \text{ .}$$

bodylist.description:
The difference of $A$ and $B$ is the set

$$A - B = \{x \mid x \in A \text{ and } x \notin B\} \text{ .}}$$

inputs.contents:
The set $A$ is a subset of $B$, written $A \subset B$, if every element of $A$ is also an element of $B$. Finally, the complement of $A$ is the set

$$\tilde{A} = \{x \mid x \in \Omega \text{ and } x \notin A\} \text{ .}$$

bodylist.description: The set $A$ is a subset of $B$, written $A \subset B$, if every element of $A$ is also an element of $B$.

inputs.contents: **Definition 1.1** Suppose we have an experiment whose outcome depends on chance. We represent the outcome of the experiment by a capital Roman letter, such as $X$, called a *random variable*. The *sample space* of the experiment is the set of all possible outcomes. If the sample space is either finite or countably infinite, the random variable is said to be *discrete*. $\square$

bodylist.description: **Definition 1.1** Suppose we have an experiment whose outcome depends on chance.
We represent the outcome of the experiment by a capital Roman letter, such as $X$, called a *random variable*. The *sample space* of the experiment is the set of all possible outcomes.
If the sample space is either finite or countably infinite, the random variable is said to be *discrete*. $\square$

inputs.contents:
**Definition 1.2**

Let $X$ be a random variable which denotes the value of the outcome of a certain experiment, and assume that this experiment has only finitely many possible outcomes. Let $\Omega$ be the sample space of the experiment (i.e., the set of all possible values of $X$, or equivalently, the set of all possible outcomes of the experiment.) A *distribution function* for $X$ is a real-valued function $m$ whose domain is $\Omega$ and which satisfies:

1. $m(\omega) \geq 0$, for all $\omega \in \Omega$, and
2. $\sum_{\omega \in \Omega} m(\omega) = 1$.

For any subset $E$ of $\Omega$, we define the *probability* of $E$ to be the number $P(E)$ given by

$$P(E) = \sum_{\omega \in E} m(\omega).$$

$\square$

A *distribution function* for $X$ is a real-valued function $m$ whose domain is $\Omega$ and which satisfies:

For any subset $E$ of $\Omega$, we define the *probability* of $E$ to be the number $P(E)$ given by

$$P(E) = \sum_{\omega \in E} m(\omega).$$

$\square$

inputs.contents:
The intersection of $A$ and $B$ is the set

$$A \cap B = \{x \mid x \in A \text{ and } x \in B\} \text{ .}$$
